# Study of Existing Solutions

## Introduction

This chapter analyses the existing solutions for academic management and, more specifically,
for PFE management. We begin by establishing the conceptual framework of the PFE within
the Algerian university system, describing its actors and process. We then survey the tools
available on the market (LMS, dedicated platforms) and conduct an objective comparative
study. A case study on the practices in use at the ESST allows us to measure the gap
between what exists and what is actually needed. Finally, the synthesis of these findings
justifies the proposal of a new dedicated solution.


## The PFE in the Algerian university system

### Nature and status by programme

The nature and importance of the PFE differ according to the level of study:

- **In the Licence programme:** the PFE is compulsory in certain specialisations. It is
  treated as a graded module within semester 6 (S6) and constitutes a full teaching unit
  (UE). Its duration is generally a few months and its workload is governed by the
  regulatory texts of the MESRS [@mesrs2004 ; @mesrs2005guide].

- **In the Master programme:** the PFE is compulsory and its grade represents the entirety
  of the evaluation for the final semester (S10). It involves a significantly greater
  workload, often linked to a company or a research laboratory. It gives rise to the writing
  of a complete thesis and a formal defence before a jury [@mesrs2004].

- **In the Engineering programme:** the PFE is also compulsory and occupies, in the
  majority of universities, the final semester of the programme (S10), while the entire
  fifth year is dedicated to the PFE in other universities or schools. It is generally
  carried out in a company or within a research laboratory, and involves solving a concrete
  technical problem related to the student's specialisation. It gives rise to the writing
  of a thesis and a defence before a mixed jury composed of lecturers and professionals
  [@mesrs2004].

| Programme     | PFE Status            | Approximate Duration | Evaluation Method                |
|---            |---                    |---                   |---                               |
| Licence (LMD) | Module of S6          | 2 to 4 months        | Thesis + defence                 |
| Master (LMD)  | Full semester (S10)   | 5 to 6 months        | Thesis + defence before jury     |
| Engineering   | Full semester (S10)   | 5 to 6 months        | Thesis + defence before jury     |

: PFE status by university programme in Algeria [@mesrs2004] {tbl-colwidths="[25,25,20,30]"}

### Types of PFE

According to the context in which it is carried out, a PFE can be classified into two
main categories [@mesrs2004]:

- **Internal PFE:** carried out within the university institution itself, under the
  supervision of a lecturer-researcher or a member of the administration (e.g. a
  department head). It generally addresses an academic problem or a direct application of
  the teaching received.

- **External PFE (or company-based):** carried out in an organisation external to the
  university (private company, public body, administration...). The student is then
  supervised both by a professional from the company (industrial supervisor or placement
  tutor) and by a lecturer (academic supervisor or co-supervisor).


## Actors of the PFE and their respective roles

The completion of a PFE is a collaborative process involving several stakeholders whose
roles are complementary and interdependent [@mesrs2004].

### The student

The student is the principal actor of the PFE. They initiate the search for a topic and
are responsible for carrying out the work [@mesrs2004]. Their responsibilities include:

- Identifying and choosing a PFE topic in line with their specialisation and interests.
- Contacting lecturers who may supervise them, or responding to proposals made by
  lecturers or companies.
- Respecting the schedule defined with the supervisor and producing regular deliverables.
- Writing a structured thesis in accordance with the institution's standards.
- Preparing and presenting the oral defence before the jury.
- Complying with the rules and objectives of the PFE.
- Maintaining regular progress in their work.

### The supervisor

The academic supervisor plays the role of pedagogical and scientific mentor [@mesrs2005guide].

They may be:

- **A lecturer (Internal):** a lecturer internal to the institution.
- **A company representative (External):** a professional from the company or organisation
  where the PFE is being carried out.

Their responsibilities consist of:

- Proposing PFE topics.
- Validating the relevance and feasibility of other PFE topics.
- Providing regular monitoring of work progress (periodic meetings, corrections...).
- Guiding the student in their methodological and scientific approach.
- Evaluating the quality of the submitted thesis and participating in the defence jury.
- Evaluating the overall execution of the PFE.

### The administration

The university administration (academic office, department, studies directorate) plays a
fundamental logistical and regulatory role [@loi9905]. As illustrated by @fig-acteurs,
it sits at the crossroads of interactions between the student and the supervisor:

- Proposing PFE topics.
- Defining and communicating the rules and procedures relating to the PFE (deadlines,
  thesis format, jury composition...).
- Collecting and validating topic proposals and supervisor-student assignments.
- Scheduling defences and forming juries.
- Archiving theses and updating results in university information systems.
- Regularly evaluating the progress status of ongoing PFE projects.

![The three PFE actors and their mutual interactions @esst](/images/trois-acteurs.png){#fig-acteurs width=55% fig-align="center"}


## Stages of the PFE process

The completion of a PFE follows a process structured in several successive phases
[@mesrs2004]. This process, though variable from one institution to another, broadly
follows these stages:

1. **Topic proposal and search:** the student searches for topics by consulting lecturers
   in their department, offers from partner companies, or announcements circulated by the
   administration. In parallel, some lecturers or companies submit topic proposals directly
   to the administration.
2. **Matching and assignment:** once the topic is identified, the student is put in contact
   with the prospective supervisor. In the case of an external PFE, a tripartite agreement
   (student - company - university) is formalised [@mesrs2004].
3. **Topic validation:** the proposed topic is submitted for validation to the pedagogical
   coordinator or department head. This step ensures the coherence of the PFE with the
   student's level of study and specialisation [@mesrs2005guide].
4. **Project work:** the student conducts the planned activities (analysis, design,
   development, experimentation...) under the supervision of their supervisor, according
   to a schedule defined in advance.
5. **Thesis writing:** the student writes a formal document describing the problem, the
   methodology adopted, the results obtained, and the perspectives envisaged. This document
   is reviewed and corrected by the supervisor [@mesrs2004].
6. **Defence:** the student presents their work before a jury composed of lecturers and,
   in the case of an external PFE, a representative of the company. A grade is awarded,
   taking into account both the written thesis and the quality of the oral presentation
   [@mesrs2004].

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-workflow-pfe
%%| fig-cap: "Workflow of the PFE process: from proposal to defence @mesrs2004"
%%| fig-width: 6.5

flowchart LR
    A([Topic Proposal]) --> B([Supervisor Assignment])
    B --> C{Topic Validation}
    C -- Rejected --> A
    C -- Accepted --> D([Project Work])
    D --> E([Thesis Writing])
    E --> F([Defence])
```


## Case study: PFE management at the ESST

In order to ground our analysis in a concrete institutional reality and to complement the
panorama of existing solutions with field observation, we conducted a case study on the
PFE management practices in use at the Ecole Superieure des Sciences et de la Technologie
(ESST) in Algiers.

### Presentation of the ESST

The ESST is an Algerian university institution under the MESRS. It trains students in
computer science and other specialisations such as pharmaceutical chemistry, electronics,
and more, under the LMD system, and awards Licence (3 years) and Master (2 additional
years) degrees @esst. Like the majority of mid-sized institutions in Algeria, the ESST
does not currently have a dedicated digital platform for managing its pedagogical and
administrative activities.

### Current PFE management process at the ESST

The observations presented below were gathered through interviews conducted with lecturers
and internal stakeholders at the ESST.

**From the student's perspective**

A student who must complete their PFE has access to no centralised catalogue of available
topics. They must therefore go around the department's **lecturers** to identify those
who propose a topic or who agree to supervise one. They may also choose to search for a
topic outside the institution, through **companies** or organisations. This search is
carried out individually, often informally (direct contact in the corridors, sending
personal emails, phone calls), with no structured framework. The student is thus heavily
dependent on their personal network of contacts within or outside the institution, which
creates inequalities in access to information.

Once a preliminary agreement is reached (generally following a verbal agreement or an
exchange of emails), the formalisation procedure begins. After the PFE form is distributed
to all students, the student must fill it in (see @fig-fiche-pfe) and submit it to the
supervisor for signature. Once signed, this form is physically submitted to the
administration for registration. It is then subject to a validation process before the
assignment is officially confirmed.

![The PFE Form used at the ESST](/images/FichePFE-improved.png){#fig-fiche-pfe width="100%"}

Once the supervisor's agreement is confirmed and the PFE officially launched, the student
is required to document each working session by completing a progress report (see
@fig-compte-rendu-pfe). This document constitutes a formal record of each meeting (whether
in person or remote) by recording essential information such as the date, duration, and a
summary of the points discussed.

**From the lecturers' perspective**

Lecturers may be approached directly by students or may themselves propose PFE topics, but
these exchanges remain informal and non-centralised. In the absence of a dedicated system,
they have no global visibility over requests or their supervision workload, which can lead
to an imbalance in the distribution of students and a lack of transparency in topic
allocation. For traceability purposes, the lecturer also fills in an identical copy of the
PFE Progress Form, thus keeping a copy on the supervisor's side (see @fig-compte-rendu-pfe).

It also happens that a lecturer proposes a topic without having previously contacted any
students, which can lead to topics going unclaimed due to insufficient interest or
visibility.

![The Progress Form at the ESST](/images/FicheSuivi-B.png){#fig-compte-rendu-pfe width="100%"}

**From the companies' perspective**

When a company wishes to propose a PFE topic to ESST students, it must contact certain
members of the administration (management, pedagogical coordinators) directly by email or
phone. Standardised forms exist, but there is no online submission portal for these
proposals. This means that company offers are not systematically communicated to all
concerned students, generating an unequal distribution of opportunities.

Conversely, when a student contacts a company themselves to complete their PFE there, it
is the company that chooses and supervises the topic. The company then passes a standardised
form to the student, who must fill it in and submit it to the administration.

**From the administration's perspective**

The administrative management of PFE projects relies entirely on standard office tools:
supervisor-student assignments are recorded in Excel spreadsheets (see
@fig-situation-globale), topic validations are communicated by email or on paper, and
defence schedules are communicated via notice boards or group emails. There is no system
allowing real-time tracking of PFE progress or automatic generation of official documents
(certificates, defence minutes...).

The validation of a PFE topic constitutes a key step in this process. Once the PFE form
is submitted by the student, the PFE Coordinator (department head or pedagogical
coordinator) forwards it to two lecturers belonging to the same scientific domain as the
proposed topic. These two lecturers examine the topic sequentially: the first gives their
opinion, then the form is passed to the second. At each step, three decisions are possible:
the topic is validated without conditions, rejected and returned for revision, or
conditionally accepted (e.g. topic **too detailed**, topic unclear, out of context, etc.)
(meaning it is conditionally approved but cannot be officially used until all stated
conditions have been resolved). Only after definitive validation by both lecturers, with
no outstanding conditions, is the assignment officially confirmed.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-validation-pfe
%%| fig-width: 6.3
%%| fig-cap: "PFE topic validation process at the ESST"

flowchart LR
    A([PFE Topic Submission]) --> B([PFE Coordinator])
    B --> C{Decision <br/> Prof. 1 & 2}
    C -- Validated --> D([Assignment Confirmed])
    C -- Rejected --> R([Revision])
    C -- Conditionally Accepted --> SR([Conditions to Clear])
    SR --> L{Conditions <br/> cleared?}
    L -- Yes --> D
    L -- No --> SR
    R --> A
```

At the end of the defence, the grading is recorded in a standardised **grading form**.
Figures `\ref{fig-notation-licence-1}`{=latex} and
`\ref{fig-notation-licence-2}`{=latex} present the front and back of the model
used for PFE grading (for Licence and Master): the front collects project information
(students, topic, supervisor, jury) and the grades awarded according to four criteria
(writing, presentation, responses to questions, quality of results), while the back records
the final decision (passed, deferred), the archiving arrangements, and any reservations
expressed by the jury.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio,page=1]{images/Fiche de notation PFE Licence 2026_cropped.pdf}
\caption{PFE Grading Form - Licence and Master (front)}
\label{fig-notation-licence-1}
\end{figure}
```


```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[htbp]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth,keepaspectratio,page=2]{images/Fiche de notation PFE Licence 2026_cropped.pdf}
\caption{PFE Grading Form - Licence and Master (back)}
\label{fig-notation-licence-2}
\end{figure}
```

![Global Status File @esst](/images/FicheGlobale-A.png){#fig-situation-globale fig-align="center" width="100%"}

**Overall process overview**

@fig-processus-esst synthesises the entire current PFE management process at the ESST,
highlighting the interactions between the different actors.

![Current PFE process at the ESST @esst](/images/etude-cas-esst-pfe-new3.png){#fig-processus-esst fig-align="center" width="81%" fig-pos="H"}

### Tools used internally

| Process step | Tool used | Observed limitations |
|---|---|---|
| Topic search by student | Direct contact (word of mouth) | Unstructured, unequal access |
| Topic proposal by a company | Email / Phone | No traceability, no centralisation |
| Topic validation | Email + paper | Slow, risk of information loss |
| Supervisor-student assignment | Excel spreadsheet | No real-time updates |
| Progress monitoring | Informal meetings | No traceability |
| Thesis submission | Physical handover / Email | No structured digital archiving |
| Defence scheduling | Notice board / Group email | Non-centralised communication |

: Tools used in PFE management at the ESST and their limitations {tbl-colwidths="[30,30,40]"}

<!-- Here are some examples of the figures (...esst fiches) -->


## Learning Management Systems (LMS)

All these findings highlight the structural limitations of management that relies on
non-integrated tools and informal processes. To address these shortcomings, it is
appropriate to examine the existing digital solutions that were designed precisely to
centralise and automate the management of academic activities -- foremost among which are
Learning Management Systems (LMS).

*Learning Management Systems* (LMS) are software platforms designed to facilitate the
creation, delivery, and tracking of educational content. Emerging in the 1990s, they
quickly became established in global higher education as central tools for digital academic
management.

@ellis2009 defines an LMS as software enabling the management and distribution of online
learning activities to multiple participants simultaneously, offering tracking, assessment,
and communication functionalities. Although initially designed for e-learning, LMS have
progressively been extended to other uses, including the management of academic projects.


### Moodle

Moodle (*Modular Object-Oriented Dynamic Learning Environment*) is the most widely used
open-source LMS in the world. Initially developed by Martin Dougiamas in 2002 and
published under the GNU GPL licence, it today counts more than 400 million users in over
230 countries [@moodle2023 ; @moodle2023stats ; @moodle2022stats]. Its modular
architecture allows extensive customisation through an ecosystem of community-developed
plugins.

In the Algerian university context, Moodle is used in several institutions as a support
for teaching (course uploads, online assignments, forums). Some universities have attempted
to adapt its assignment management modules for PFE tracking, with mixed results: the tool
remains generalist and does not model the specific stages of the Algerian PFE process
(topic proposal, validation, assignment).

Key Moodle features relevant to PFE management:

- User management (roles: student, lecturer, administrator).
- File upload and exchange (theses, progress reports).
- Internal messaging and discussion forum system.
- Grade and feedback assignment.
- Student activity tracking.

### Other notable LMS

Beyond Moodle, other LMS are used in global higher education, each with its own
characteristics:

- **Canvas (Instructure):** a proprietary LMS widely used in North America (approximately
  41% of the North American market [@hill2024]), appreciated for its modern and intuitive
  interface. It offers advanced collaboration features and integrates easily with
  third-party tools. However, it offers no dedicated mechanism for PFE management and its
  cost makes it largely inaccessible to Algerian institutions.
- **Blackboard Learn:** one of the first commercial LMS, widely adopted by large
  universities. It offers advanced analytical tools but its complexity and high cost make
  it poorly suited to the context of mid-sized Algerian institutions.
- **Google Classroom:** a lightweight, free solution from Google, particularly suited to
  secondary education and small structures. Its integration with the Google ecosystem
  (Drive, Docs, Meet) is its main asset. However, its features remain too limited to cover
  a process as structured as the PFE.
- **Microsoft Teams for Education:** a Microsoft solution integrating class management,
  communication, and collaboration, increasingly adopted in higher education thanks to the
  free licences offered to institutions. It offers no topic management, assignment, or
  validation features, however, making it insufficient for complete PFE monitoring.

### Other tools used in the academic context

Beyond LMS, some Algerian university institutions use general-purpose office and
collaboration suites to compensate for the absence of a dedicated platform. Among the most
widely used:

- **Google Workspace (Google Drive, Docs, Meet):** used informally for document sharing,
  remote communication, and work monitoring. Its free accessibility and ease of use make
  it a frequent recourse, although it offers no features specific to PFE management.
- **Microsoft 365 (Word, Excel, Teams):** widely used in Algerian administrations, it is
  often used for managing assignments (Excel), writing theses (Word), and communication
  (Teams or Outlook email). As with Google Workspace, its use in this context constitutes
  a workaround rather than an integration designed for the PFE process.

These tools, although useful on a day-to-day basis, do not constitute solutions adapted to
the structured management of a process as specific as the PFE. Their fragmented use
contributes to the lack of centralisation and traceability already identified at the ESST.


## Comparative study of existing solutions

In order to objectively evaluate the solutions identified in the previous section, a
comparative study was conducted by defining a set of functional and non-functional criteria
directly related to PFE management needs.

### Evaluation criteria

The criteria selected for comparison derive directly from the needs identified
during the study of the PFE process and the dysfunctions observed at the ESST. Each
criterion corresponds to an essential requirement for managing the PFE lifecycle:

- **User and role management**: actors with distinct responsibilities requiring fine-grained
  access rights management.
- **Topic proposal and assignment**: the founding step of the process, currently
  unstructured (cf. section II.4).
- **Progress monitoring**: the absence of traceability in pedagogical monitoring is a major
  dysfunction identified at the ESST.
- **Integrated communication**: coordination across disparate channels, a source of
  information loss.
- **Document management**: physical submission guarantees neither structured archiving nor
  traceability.
- **Adaptation to the Algerian context**: MESRS regulations and local defence practices
  not covered by generalist solutions.
- **Cost and accessibility**: limited budgets of mid-sized institutions.
- **Ease of deployment**: frequent absence of dedicated IT teams.


### Comparison table

| Criterion                      |   Moodle  |   Canvas  | Blackboard | Identified requirements |
| ------------------------------ | :-------: | :-------: | :--------: | :---------------------: |
| Role management                |     v     |     v     |      v     |       Mandatory         |
| Topic proposal                 | Partial   |     x     |      x     |       Mandatory         |
| Supervisor-student assignment  | Manual    | Manual    |  Manual    |       Automated         |
| Progress monitoring            | Partial   | Partial   |  Partial   |       Mandatory         |
| Integrated communication       |     v     |     v     |      v     |       Desired           |
| Document management            |     v     |     v     |      v     |       Mandatory         |
| Adapted to Algerian context    |     x     |     x     |      x     |       Mandatory         |
| Open-source / Free             |     v     |     x     |      x     |       Preferred         |
| Simple deployment              | Medium    | Difficult |  Difficult |       Simple            |

: Comparison of existing solutions and requirements identified for the proposed solution {tbl-colwidths="[25,15,15,15,15,15]"}

### Analysis of results

This comparative analysis shows that the generalist LMS available on the market (Moodle,
Canvas, Blackboard) cover communication and document management needs adequately
[@cavus2013], but prove insufficient when it comes to managing the PFE process in its
entirety. In particular, none of these solutions offers an automated mechanism for the
proposal, assignment, and validation of PFE topics.

Furthermore, these solutions take no account whatsoever of the specificities of the
Algerian university system, notably: the dual validation of topics by two lecturers from
the same domain (with the possibility of conditional acceptance), assignment based on
ranked preferences, the formation of juries according to MESRS rules, and grading out of
20 distributed across five specific criteria (four for the jury, one for the supervisor).
Their deployment in this context would require adaptations costly in time and human
resources.


## Synthesis of limitations and problem statement

The comparative study of LMS and the field observation at the ESST converge on the same
finding: despite the central role played by the PFE in the validation of university
programmes [@loi9905], its management remains largely artisanal and unstructured today
[@insaniyat2018 ; @berrouche2007].

### Identified dysfunctions

The three actors involved (student, supervisor, and administration) have at their disposal
no unified platform to coordinate the different stages of the process. Exchanges rely
primarily on standard office tools (Microsoft Word, Microsoft Excel), email communication,
or direct telephone contact.

This lack of automation generates several recurring dysfunctions [@berrouche2007]:

- Risk of errors (manual data entry) that can affect data quality.
- Informal archiving through Google Drive and Google Sheets, with unordered data.
- Forms can be lost or damaged, causing delays in the validation process.
- The administration may archive documents incorrectly, leading to information loss.
- Difficulty for students in identifying available topics and potential supervisors, due
  to the absence of a centralised and up-to-date list.
- Lack of traceability of exchanges and validations between stakeholders.
- Administrative overload on pedagogical coordinators and secretariats, linked to the
  manual processing of numerous files.
- Risks of errors, information loss, and missed deadlines in the absence of automated
  monitoring.
- The impossibility of obtaining reliable, real-time statistics on PFE progress at the
  institutional level.

These dysfunctions crystallise into four structural axes:

- **Lack of centralisation:** information relating to topics, supervisors, and defences is
  scattered across multiple media (spreadsheets, emails, notice boards), with no single
  point of access for stakeholders.
- **Absence of automation:** each step of the process requires manual human intervention,
  generating a high administrative workload and risks of errors.
- **Poor traceability:** the history of exchanges, validations, and decisions is not
  systematically preserved in a searchable and structured form.
- **Inequalities in access to information:** students who are less well connected to the
  institution's informal network are disadvantaged in their search for a topic and a
  supervisor.

## Positioning and justification of the proposed solution

The analysis conducted throughout this chapter (study of generalist LMS, comparative study,
and case study at the ESST) converges on a clear finding: there is currently no dedicated
software solution, adapted to the Algerian context, capable of managing the entire
lifecycle of a Final Year Project in an integrated and automated manner.

Existing LMS (Moodle, Canvas...) cover general pedagogical needs but do not model the PFE
process in its specificity. Local solutions constitute partial attempts that do not cover
all stages. And the reality on the ground at the ESST illustrates the real costs of this
absence: information loss, organisational inefficiency, and inequalities between students.

It is in this context that the contribution of this thesis is situated: the design and
development of a web application dedicated to PFE management, targeting specifically
Algerian university institutions and, as a priority, the ESST. This application proposes to:

- Centralise on a single platform all information relating to PFE projects (topics,
  assignments, schedules, defences).
- Automate the key steps of the process (proposals, validations, notifications).
- Offer each actor (student, lecturer, administration) an interface adapted to their needs
  and role.
- Ensure complete traceability of exchanges and decisions throughout the PFE lifecycle.

> **Note:** The proposed solution is not an adapted generalist LMS, but a dedicated
> business application, custom-built to meet the real and specific needs of PFE management
> in the Algerian university context.

The following chapters will describe in detail the design of this solution: requirements
analysis, modelling, technical architecture, and implementation.

## Conclusion

This chapter provided a complete and progressive panorama of existing solutions in the
field of digital academic management. Starting from a definition of the conceptual
framework of the PFE and its actors, we surveyed and compared the LMS available on the
market, before concretely observing their limitations through the ESST case study. The
synthesis of these findings highlighted four major axes of dysfunction -- lack of
centralisation, absence of automation, poor traceability, and inequalities in access --
which constitute the fundamental justification for the project presented in this thesis.
The functional and non-functional requirements that the proposed solution must meet derive
directly from this analysis.
